context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi! I’m working on Problem Set 9 for 6.1200, and I’m pretty stuck on the probability parts, especially Problem 3. I think I kind of get the sample space idea, but I’m not confident about setting up the events and counting them correctly.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's dig in. Before I can help effectively, can you tell me specifically which part of Problem 3 you're working on, and show me what you've tried so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a vague opening statement. Before providing any scaffolding, I should ask them to be more specific about where they are stuck, and get them to show me what they've already tried. This respects the Socratic method and guided discovery - I shouldn't jump in with hints until I know where they actually are.

exchanges.student: I was looking at 3(c) and 3(f). For 3(c), I think the complement of “sum is at least 18” is “sum is at most 17,” but I’m not seeing the bijection clearly. For 3(f), I’m getting mixed up between “at least two match” and “at least three match,” and I’m not sure how to count those without overcounting.
exchanges.tutor:
Good - you've correctly identified the complement. Now let's think about the bijection for 3(c).

You have an outcome in A18 (five dice summing to at least 18) and you need to map it to an outcome in the complement (five dice summing to at most 17). 

Here's a question to get you started: if you have a single die showing value x, what's a natural "mirror" value you could map it to that stays on a standard die?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has two problems they're working on. I should focus on one at a time to avoid overwhelming them. 3(c) seems more foundational and they've already articulated the complement correctly, so I'll start there. I want to probe their thinking about the bijection with a Socratic question rather than giving any hints about what the bijection is.

exchanges.student: Maybe map \(x\) to \(7-x\)? Since that sends 1↔6, 2↔5, 3↔4. I think that would make the total sum become \(35 - \text{old sum}\), but I’m not fully sure how that proves it lands exactly in the complement.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. So if the old sum S is at least 18, what can you say about 35 - S? Does it land in the complement?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the mapping x → 7-x and correctly computed that the total sum transforms to 35 - old sum. Now I should push them to use this fact to verify the bijection lands in the complement, rather than explaining it myself. One targeted Socratic question should do it.

exchanges.student: If \(S \ge 18\), then \(35-S \le 17\), so yeah, it lands in the complement. I guess I also need the reverse direction to show it’s actually a bijection, and maybe that applying it twice gives the original outcome back?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — if the map is its own inverse, that's sufficient to conclude it's a bijection. Now, what does the existence of this bijection tell you about the sizes of A18 and its complement? And how does that help you compute Pr[A18]?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned about both directions and intuited that the map is its own inverse (an involution), which is exactly what makes it a bijection. I should affirm this and push them to now connect this to computing Pr[A18], rather than dwelling further on the bijection.

exchanges.student: I think it means \(|A_{18}| = |A_{18}^c|\). Since every outcome in the sample space is equally likely, that should mean their probabilities are equal too, so each one is \(1/2\). So \(\Pr[A_{18}] = 1/2\).
exchanges.tutor: Well done — that's exactly right. Now let's move to 3(f). You mentioned getting mixed up between D2 and D3. Can you tell me in your own words what the events D2 and D3 mean, and what Pr[D3 | D2] is asking?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly concluded Pr[A18] = 1/2 using the bijection argument. This is correct and well-reasoned. Now I should move them to Problem 3(f) since that's the other problem they mentioned struggling with, and probe their current understanding before scaffolding.

exchanges.student:
I think \(D_2\) means at least two dice show the same value, and \(D_3\) means at least three dice show the same value. So \(\Pr[D_3\mid D_2]\) is like: given that there is some repeated value somewhere, what’s the chance that actually some value appears 3 or more times.

I’m a little worried \(D_2\) might just always happen with 5 dice and only 6 values, but I want to check that instead of assuming it.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — don't assume, check it. Can you think of a concrete outcome where no two dice show the same value? That will tell you whether D2 always happens or not.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly understood D2 and D3, and is showing good mathematical instinct by wanting to verify whether D2 always occurs rather than assuming it. I should encourage this and ask them to actually check it — this is good Socratic engagement. I won't confirm or deny whether D2 always happens; I'll let them figure it out.

exchanges.student: Wait, yeah — like \((1,2,3,4,5)\). So \(D_2\) doesn’t always happen. Then I guess I need \(\Pr[D_3 \cap D_2] / \Pr[D_2]\), but since \(D_3 \subseteq D_2\), that should just be \(\Pr[D_3]/\Pr[D_2]\), right?
exchanges.tutor: Correct on all counts. Now comes the counting work. Let's take one at a time — how would you count the outcomes in D2 (at least two dice show the same value)? Do you want to count directly, or use a complement approach?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified a counterexample, correctly set up the conditional probability formula, and correctly applied the subset relationship D3 ⊆ D2 to simplify. This is solid reasoning. Now I should push them to actually compute Pr[D3] and Pr[D2], which is where the real counting challenge lies. I'll ask them to tackle one of these first.

exchanges.student:
Complement seems easier. I think \(D_2^c\) is “all five dice are different,” so that would be \(6\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 3\cdot 2\) outcomes out of \(6^5\). So \(\Pr[D_2]=1-\frac{6\cdot5\cdot4\cdot3\cdot2}{6^5}\).

For \(D_3\), I’m tempted to also use a complement, but then I think I’d have to count “no value appears 3 or more times,” and that feels messy.
exchanges.tutor: Good work on D2. For D3, since the complement is messy, think about counting D3 directly. What are the different "shapes" or patterns an outcome with at least 3 matching dice could have? Try listing the cases by how the dice values are distributed.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed Pr[D2] using the complement. For D3, they're right that the complement is messier. I should guide them toward a direct counting approach for D3 using cases, without telling them what the cases are. A gentle Socratic nudge toward case analysis is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: I think the cases are \(3+1+1\), \(3+2\), \(4+1\), and \(5\). I’m not totally sure if that’s all of them, but I think those are the only ways to have some value appear at least 3 times with 5 dice.
exchanges.tutor: That's the complete list — nicely done. Now pick the case you feel most confident about and try to count the number of outcomes in that case. Show me your reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all four cases for D3. I should confirm this is complete and push them to count one of the cases to get them moving, rather than confirming all cases and then letting them stall.

exchanges.student:
I’ll try the \(5\) case first: all five dice the same, so that should be just \(6\) outcomes.

For \(4+1\): choose the value that appears four times in \(6\) ways, choose the different value in \(5\) ways, and choose which die is the odd one out in \(5\) ways, so I get \(6\cdot 5\cdot 5\).

I think that’s right, but I’m less confident about \(3+2\) versus \(3+1+1\).
exchanges.tutor: Good progress. For 3+2 and 3+1+1, the key question is the same: how many ways are there to choose which dice show which values? Try setting up the count for 3+2 step by step — how many choices do you have at each stage?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly counted the '5' case and the '4+1' case. Now I should push them to tackle 3+2 and 3+1+1 themselves, using a Socratic question to guide their thinking about choosing which dice get which values. I should not validate or correct their 4+1 count explicitly - let them work through the next cases and they can cross-check at the end.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated excellent Socratic technique throughout the conversation, consistently asking targeted questions rather than providing answers, building on student progress at each step, and keeping the student doing the key reasoning work. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave away answers or near-submission-ready content. Every turn posed a guiding question that required the student to reason independently (e.g., 'what can you say about 35-S?', 'try listing the cases'). The student generated all key ideas (7-x map, complement argument, case enumeration). 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student steps before advancing, and used diagnostic questions (Turn 1, Turn 6) before addressing confusion. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback where relevant (e.g., suggesting complement vs. direct counting as a strategic choice in Turn 8). 2.1-2.2: The conversation progressed steadily without repetition or drift. 3.1-3.2: Responses were concise, focused, and supportive throughout, with no monologues or tone issues.
